criteria_results.extracted_evidence: Women's Goose Society performed maize rituals; Bear Society members danced like bears; transformation implied but limited detail.
Medicine Pipe Society required holding feasts; Oglala societies gave food/goods; feasts prerequisite for membership.
Sacred bundles and medicine bags mentioned but no specific exotic non-local materials described for Arikara specifically.
No specific liminal site locations described for Arikara ritual spaces in this text.
Sequential ranked societies with hereditary-like roles and institutional structure implied across generations for Plains groups broadly.
No information on mortuary practices or burial locations relative to ritual sites provided in text.
Leader Lodge mentioned as distinct structure; ceremonial lodges implied but not architecturally described for Arikara specifically.
No mention of remote locations or isolated camps used for Arikara initiation or ritual seclusion.
Sacred war bundles, medicine bags, sacred bundles kept secret; bundle-based societies with esoteric powers explicitly described.
Bear Society, Buffalo Society, animal patrons; Iowa Animal Society uses animal patrons; bears, buffalo spirits depicted ritually.
Society of Chiefs spans thirteen Pawnee villages; shared bundle-society forms across Arikara, Pawnee, Mandan, Iowa, Omaha, Blackfoot.
Bear, wolf, eagle spirit possessions; cannibalistic transformations; spirit patrons characterize society dances among Bella Coola.
Feasts opened winter season, given after all Kusiut performances, at initiation culmination; best food always provided.
Crystals mentioned as shot into initiates and removed by marshals; regalia acquired via marriage dowry, but limited detail.
No specific mention of liminal site locations for Kusiut/Sisaok ritual spaces in the text.
Hereditary positions, repeated initiations across generations, annual winter ceremonials; marshals maintained multi-village policing across generations.
Cave burials mentioned generally for Northwest Coast; no specific Kusiut/Sisaok burial location data provided.
Dance house referenced for performances; candidate's family house used for feasts, but dedicated ceremonial structure not explicitly described.
Chiefs' sons underwent repeated seclusions; novices went on spirit quests, but specific remote locations not detailed for Bella Coola.
Secret society knowledge strictly guarded; revealing secrets punishable by raids; marshals policed supernatural fictions from non-initiates.
Bear, wolf, eagle as most powerful animals, hungering for human flesh; imaginary sea monsters like Sisiutl featured in iconography.
Invitations sent to Nass River and Alert Bay; Kusiut members accepted across villages; inter-tribal marshal cooperation explicitly stated.
Masks worn to impersonate spirits; members transform into bears; katsina masks enable human-spirit boundary crossing
Feasting mentioned as typical ritual feature; cooks listed as specialist roles; feasting at ceremonies documented
Shells (abalone, olivella, cowrie, conch), quartz crystals, and exotic materials used in restricted ritual contexts
Masks kept in sipapu cave six miles from pueblo; shrines at water margins, caves, and remote locations deliberately used
Hereditary clan ownership of kivas and masks; multi-generational use of kivas and society houses maintained institutionally
Text mentions deceased members living on in masks but does not directly address burial separation from ritual loci
Kivas and society houses purpose-built for restricted ritual use, distinct from residential architecture
Shrines and caves at 6 to 80 miles from villages used for pilgrimages and private ceremonies by secret society leaders
Most sacred items cached, rarely seen, highly curated; masks stored in remote caves; fetishes seen only by ordained priests
Bear iconography prominent; stone animal sculptures as society patrons; cougar and snake designs on ceramics; bear shamans as enforcers
Shared katsina cult, shell networks, and ritual practices documented across multiple Pueblo groups and linguistic boundaries
Spirit costumes with animal skins, horns, hooves; bear/Kuksu impersonators; masked dances across multiple groups
Elite feasting with stone ollas/mortars; long feasting tables near dance houses; inter-community ritual feasts
Crystals, shells, obsidian, feathers, mountain lion claws, hawk beaks/claws in sacred bundles and costumes
Semi-subterranean dance houses; caves used for rituals; bush houses in hills; mountaintop shrines
Hereditary roles and memberships; dance houses inherited; multi-generational use of same ritual locations
One Patwin dance house held 30 cremations; Maidu secret society members cremated separately from others
Large earth-covered dance houses, sacred enclosures, men's houses, secret society headquarters distinct from residences
Initiations 'in the hills,' mountaintop shrines, bush dance houses 200-300m outside villages, caves for rituals
Ritual paraphernalia stored in caves, rock shelters, chief's residence; sacred bundles kept secret from non-initiates
Bear, mountain lion, condor, eagle, hawk iconography; bear impersonators; coyote skins as altar containers
Kuksu Cult integrated hundreds of communities across language groups; ritual purchase/spread between villages; regional networks
Terror masks used to terrify novices; high-ranking members possessed headdresses with supernatural power; spirit effigies inhabited by dead members
Candidates gave great banquets; piles of food collected; large yam gardens planted for required feasts; feasts for promotion
Shell money, wealth mats, pig tusk bracelets, stone monoliths, and carved statues required as initiation payments and paraphernalia
Seclusion in bush mentioned; initiations in clubhouse (amel); no explicit water margin or cave liminality described
Nimangki had 32 grades with hereditary sponsorship; statues housed spirits of previous grade holders including original founders
Text focuses on ancestor spirits in statues/stones but does not explicitly describe burial locations relative to ritual sites
Nimangki clubhouse (amel) used for seclusion; candidates not permitted to eat in Nimangki clubhouse; distinct ritual structure described
Higher grade initiations involved one year seclusion in amel; vast pile of food taken to bush by society members during initiations
Rank-specific paraphernalia, headdresses, statues, and monoliths held secret power; could harm uninitiated; sold with rank positions
Terror masks embodying dangerous spirits/ghosts used to terrify; ogresses that eat men; spirit effigies with harmful supernatural power
Nimangki system including songs, dances, art, and customs bought and sold between ethnic groups on Malekula; high grades introduced from other groups
Masks, costumes used to assume spirit roles; initiates said to die and return transformed; possession by ancestral spirits.
Numerous feasts on nightly basis during ritual season; grandiose feasts at sons' initiations described as greatest potlatch of career.
Quartz crystals, cowrie shells, copper nails, exotic pelts, decorated boxes among initiation wealth items used ritually.
Special meeting places 150-400 meters outside villages; caves used for seclusion and storage; no structures built at remote locations.
Hereditary roles tied to descent groups; chiefly offices linked to secret societies; multi-generational transmission of spirit powers.
Burials of high-ranking members not distinguished from chiefly burials; shamans buried in caves but uncertain if secret society members.
Ceremonies held inside community houses appropriated and rearranged for purpose; no purpose-built separate ceremonial structures reported.
Candidates taken to secluded locations outside villages for seclusion; caves used for meetings and ritual storage.
Ritual paraphernalia stored in faraway locations including rock shelters and caves; secrets kept from non-initiates on pain of death.
Bears, wolves, ravens, eagles, killer whales, thunderbirds, sea monsters emphasized as power sources; non-prey therianthropic imagery.
Northwest Coast Interaction Sphere explicitly named; shared art styles, masks, rituals across Tsimshian, Tlingit, Kwakwakawakw, Bella Coola, Nuuchahnulth.
Animal costumes with horns, fox mandibles, owl elements, coyote/raven skins with heads used in ceremonies.
Virtually all ceremonies and initiations accompanied by feasts, including some for general public.
Exotic shells and stones listed among paraphernalia used in restricted ritual contexts.
Hilltops, caves, woods locations used; tipis located in center of villages or woods at remove.
Loosely hereditary membership across generations; hereditary privileges noted; institutionalised return implied.
No mention of burial practices or mortuary activity at ritual sites in the text.
Earth lodge structures holding 500-600 people erected specifically for secret society ceremonies.
Hilltops, solitary tents, caves used for fasting and visions; tipis in woods at remove from villages.
Medicine bags and bundles, sacred paraphernalia kept secret from non-initiates; revealing society tricks caused death.
Bears, rattlesnakes, mythical water monsters, ghosts as power animal patrons; raven/coyote/eagle elements used.
Regional gatherings of 12-13 villages; some societies incorporated members from different ethnic groups.
Masks, costumes, animal personas (bears, wolves, ravens) used in transformation rituals; wolf impersonators, cannibal spirit possession documented.
Initiation feasts and winter ceremonial feasts documented; feasting in dance houses and potlatch houses mentioned.
Crystals mentioned (marshals removing crystals); copper implied via prestige regalia; explicit aggregation at restricted loci not fully described.
Secret meeting places at waterfalls, cliff bases, caves, forest edges; Rose Island outside Port Simpson used by secret societies.
Sisaok leader buried in community cemetery behind houses; some shamans buried in caves; no burials at main ritual sites described.
Dance houses mentioned but unclear if pre-contact; large residential structures adapted for ceremonies; no distinct special structures confirmed archaeologically for Tsimshian.
Tsimshian initiates secluded 4-12 days in huts or caves in bush; initiates into Shamans' dance sequestered in cave surrounded by corpses.
Ritual paraphernalia stored in hollow cedar trees, caves (Cave of Animals), locations far from habitation; masks cached at remote sites.
Bears, wolves, eagles, sea monsters, thunderbirds, killer whales depicted as threatening power animals; imaginary creatures like Sisiutl prominent in iconography.
Regional networks documented; Tsimshian invitations sent to Nass River and Alert Bay; society membership crossing tribal lines; shared iconographic motifs across Northwest Coast.
